era_context: The Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I is consolidating its control over former Parthian territories. The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces mounting military and political pressures. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is becoming firmly established following the formal collapse of the Han Dynasty. This is a pivotal year for the emergence of the Three Kingdoms as distinct political entities and the consolidation of the Sassanid Empire.

events.title: Liu Bei proclaims himself emperor and establishes Shu Han
events.description: Liu Bei, a warlord claiming descent from the Han imperial house, formally proclaims himself emperor and establishes the state of Shu Han. This proclamation marks the formal establishment of one of the Three Kingdoms and represents a direct challenge to Cao Pi's Wei and Sun Quan's Wu. Liu Bei's claim to legitimacy is based on his descent from the Han imperial family and his reputation for benevolent governance. The establishment of Shu Han solidifies the Three Kingdoms period as a stable tripartite division of China, with each state claiming legitimacy as a successor to the Han Dynasty.
key_figures: Liu Bei (founder and emperor of Shu Han)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi) by Luo Guanzhong

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's proclamation as emperor of Shu Han in 221 CE is well documented in Chinese historical records. This is one of the most securely dated events of the Three Kingdoms period.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)
Contemporary with 221-002 (Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor of Wei)

events.title: Cao Pi proclaims himself emperor and establishes Wei Dynasty
events.description: Cao Pi, son of Cao Cao and ruler of the state of Wei, formally proclaims himself emperor and establishes the Wei Dynasty. This proclamation marks the formal end of the Han Dynasty and the establishment of Wei as a legitimate imperial state. Cao Pi's proclamation is based on his military and administrative achievements and his control of the economically productive Yellow River valley. The establishment of Wei as an imperial state solidifies the Three Kingdoms period as a tripartite division of China, with each state claiming legitimacy as a successor to the Han Dynasty.
key_figures: Cao Pi (founder and emperor of Wei)
events.certainty_note: Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor of Wei in 221 CE is well documented in Chinese historical records. However, some sources date this event to 220 CE, creating minor chronological variation.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)
Contemporary with 221-001 (Liu Bei's proclamation as emperor of Shu Han)

events.title: Sassanid consolidation and administrative reforms
events.description: Ardashir I continues consolidating the Sassanid Empire's administrative structure and military organization. The Sassanid state develops a sophisticated system of provincial administration, tax collection, and military organization. Zoroastrianism is promoted as the state religion, and the Sassanid nobility is reorganized into a hierarchical system. These administrative reforms strengthen the empire's capacity for sustained military campaigns and long-term imperial governance.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid administrative consolidation is well attested in general terms, but specific events in 221 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Sassanid state-building (224-240 CE)

events.title: Roman military preparations for eastern campaign
events.description: Severus Alexander begins military preparations for a campaign against Sassanid Persia. The emperor mobilizes forces and prepares to defend Roman territories in Mesopotamia and Syria against Sassanid expansion. However, the campaign is delayed by internal political instability and military discipline problems. The preparations reflect the growing threat posed by the newly consolidated Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Roman military preparations for an eastern campaign are attested in sources, but the exact timing and scope of preparations in 221 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman-Sassanid conflict (220-240 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor is disputed. Some sources place this event in 220 CE rather than 221 CE. The exact chronology of Liu Bei's proclamation is also subject to minor variation in sources. The Historia Augusta's account of Severus Alexander's reign contains legendary material and is considered unreliable by modern scholars. The precise dating of Sassanid administrative reforms is not always clear in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: 221 CE is a pivotal year for the establishment of the Three Kingdoms as distinct political entities and the consolidation of the Sassanid Empire. Chinese history is well documented through the Sanguozhi and later compilations, which provide detailed records of the Three Kingdoms period. Roman history relies on the Historia Augusta and later historians, which are of variable reliability. Sassanid history is known primarily through later Persian sources and Roman accounts. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Liu Bei's and Cao Pi's proclamations as emperors occurred in the same year, solidifying the Three Kingdoms period
Cao Pi's establishment of Wei led to continued consolidation and military campaigns
Sassanid consolidation led to military expansion into Roman territories
Military preparations led to Severus Alexander's eastern campaign
